nodes.title: Explain why a measure of dispersion is needed beyond central tendency
nodes.brief: Recognise that two data sets can share the same mean and median yet differ greatly in spread, so a single number describing scatter — a measure of dispersion — is required.
nodes.quote: the measures of central tendency are not sufficient to give complete information about a given data.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Central tendency locates the centre but not the spread.
A measure of central tendency (mean, median, mode) tells us roughly where the data cluster. For example, both batsman A and batsman B have a mean score of 53 and median 53 over ten innings. Yet batsman A's scores range from 0 to 117 while batsman B's stay between 46 and 60 — clearly the two performers are very different. Central tendency alone cannot tell us which player is more consistent.

PART 2 — Variability is a separate feature that needs its own number.
Because two sets with the same centre can differ wildly in scatter, we need a second summary number that measures how spread out the values are. This number is called a measure of dispersion. A small value of this measure means the observations cluster tightly around the centre; a large value means they are spread far apart.

PART 3 — The four standard measures of dispersion.
The chapter studies four measures: Range (max − min), Quartile deviation, Mean deviation, and Standard deviation. Range and quartile deviation are studied only briefly; mean deviation and standard deviation (with its companion variance) are studied in detail, because they depend on every observation, not just the extremes.
keyMoves: State that mean and median locate the centre but not the spread of data
Show by example that equal mean/median can hide very different scatter (batsman A vs B)
Name the single number describing variability as a 'measure of dispersion'
misconceptions: Believing two data sets with the same mean must be equally spread
Thinking central tendency alone fully summarises a data set

nodes.title: Compute the range of a data set as maximum minus minimum
nodes.brief: Find the range, the simplest measure of dispersion, as the difference between the largest and smallest observation, and note its limitation.
nodes.quote: Thus, Range of a series = Maximum value – Minimum value.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Computing the range.
The range is the crudest measure of dispersion: Range = Maximum value − Minimum value. For batsman A the range is 117 − 0 = 117; for batsman B it is 60 − 46 = 14. A larger range means the data are more spread out.

PART 2 — The limitation of the range.
The range uses only the two extreme observations and completely ignores how the remaining values are distributed about the centre. Two data sets can have identical ranges yet very different patterns of scatter. Because of this, range does not measure dispersion from a central tendency, which is why finer measures — ones that involve every observation — are needed.
keyMoves: Identify the maximum and minimum observations in the data
Compute Range = Maximum value − Minimum value
State the limitation: it uses only the extremes, not scatter about the centre
misconceptions: Adding the extreme values instead of subtracting them
Treating the range as a full description of dispersion rather than a rough one

nodes.title: Define mean deviation about a central value as the mean of absolute deviations
nodes.brief: Define mean deviation about a value 'a' as the average of the absolute deviations |x_i − a|, and explain why absolute values are used.
nodes.quote: Thus mean deviation about a central value 'a' is the mean of the absolute values of the deviations of the observations from 'a'.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why we cannot simply average the signed deviations.
The deviation of observation x_i from a fixed value a is x_i − a. If we average the signed deviations about the arithmetic mean they sum to zero (each positive deviation is cancelled by a corresponding negative one), giving a meaningless 0 for every data set. This is why we must remove the signs before averaging.

PART 2 — Absolute deviations measure distance from the centre.
Instead of signed deviations we take the absolute value |x_i − a|, which measures the distance of x_i from a on the number line regardless of direction. All absolute deviations are non-negative, so averaging them gives a genuinely positive measure of scatter.

PART 3 — The mean deviation formula.
Mean deviation about the central value a is M.D.(a) = (1/n) times the sum of |x_i − a| for all i. It can be computed about any fixed value a, but the two standard choices are the arithmetic mean (a = x̄) and the median (a = M).
keyMoves: Form each deviation x_i − a from the chosen central value 'a'
Take absolute values |x_i − a| (so positives and negatives do not cancel)
Average them: M.D.(a) = (sum of |x_i − a|) / n
misconceptions: Averaging signed deviations (which sum to zero about the mean) instead of absolute ones
Thinking mean deviation can only be measured about the mean, not the median

nodes.title: Compute mean deviation about mean and median for ungrouped data
nodes.brief: Carry out the step-wise calculation of M.D.(x̄) and M.D.(M) for a raw list of observations.
nodes.quote: The following steps are involved in the calculation of mean deviation about mean or median:
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find the central value first.
For M.D. about the mean: compute x̄ = (x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_n)/n. For M.D. about the median: sort the n observations in ascending order; the median M is the middle value when n is odd, or the mean of the two middle values when n is even.

PART 2 — Form and sum the absolute deviations.
For each observation x_i compute |x_i − a| (where a is x̄ or M as chosen). Sum all n absolute deviations.

PART 3 — Average to get M.D.
Divide the total by n: M.D.(x̄) = (1/n) sum of |x_i − x̄|, or M.D.(M) = (1/n) sum of |x_i − M|. Example: data 6, 7, 10, 12, 13, 4, 8, 12 have mean 9 (sum = 72, n = 8); absolute deviations are 3, 2, 1, 3, 4, 5, 1, 3 summing to 22; so M.D.(x̄) = 22/8 = 2.75.
keyMoves: Compute the mean, or sort and take the median, as the central value
Form the absolute deviations |x_i − a| of every observation
Average them: M.D.(x̄) = (1/n)Σ|x_i − x̄| or M.D.(M) = (1/n)Σ|x_i − M|
misconceptions: Forgetting to sort the data before reading off the median
Dividing the summed absolute deviations by something other than the count n

nodes.title: Compute mean deviation for a discrete frequency distribution
nodes.brief: Weight each distinct value by its frequency to find M.D. about mean or median for a discrete frequency distribution.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The structure of a discrete frequency distribution.
When n distinct values x_1, x_2, ..., x_k occur with frequencies f_1, f_2, ..., f_k, the data are summarised in a frequency table. The total count is N = f_1 + f_2 + ... + f_k. The mean is x̄ = (f_1 x_1 + f_2 x_2 + ... + f_k x_k) / N.

PART 2 — Frequency-weighted absolute deviations.
For each value x_i, the term f_i times |x_i − a| counts the contribution of all f_i observations with that value. Sum these over all k values to get the total absolute deviation: sum of f_i|x_i − a|.

PART 3 — Divide by N.
M.D.(x̄) = (1/N) times the sum of f_i|x_i − x̄|. For M.D.(M), build the cumulative frequency column and identify the value whose cumulative frequency first reaches N/2; that value is the median M, then apply M.D.(M) = (1/N) sum of f_i|x_i − M|.
keyMoves: Find x̄ = (Σf_i x_i)/N, or locate the median via cumulative frequency ≥ N/2
Form frequency-weighted absolute deviations f_i|x_i − a|
Divide their sum by N = Σf_i to get M.D.(x̄) or M.D.(M)
misconceptions: Averaging the absolute deviations over the number of distinct values instead of over N = Σf_i
Reading the median straight off the values without using cumulative frequencies

nodes.title: Compute mean deviation for a continuous frequency distribution
nodes.brief: Replace each class by its mid-point (and find the median by the class formula) to compute mean deviation for grouped continuous data.
nodes.quote: A continuous frequency distribution is a series in which the data are classified into different class-intervals without gaps alongwith their respective frequencies.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Represent each class by its mid-point.
In a continuous frequency distribution data are grouped into classes such as 10–20, 20–30, etc. We assume all observations in a class are concentrated at the class mid-point: x_i = (lower limit + upper limit)/2. For a class 10–20, the mid-point is 15. With mid-points in hand the distribution is treated exactly like a discrete frequency distribution.

PART 2 — Mean and M.D. about the mean.
Compute x̄ = (1/N) sum of f_i x_i using the mid-points. Then M.D.(x̄) = (1/N) sum of f_i|x_i − x̄|.

PART 3 — Median and M.D. about the median.
For grouped data the median is found from the formula M = l + ((N/2 − C)/f) times h, where l is the lower boundary of the median class (the class whose cumulative frequency first reaches or exceeds N/2), C is the cumulative frequency of the class before it, f is the frequency of the median class, and h is the class width. Then compute M.D.(M) = (1/N) sum of f_i|x_i − M| using the mid-points.
keyMoves: Take the mid-point x_i of each class as its representative value
Use x̄ = (1/N)Σf_i x_i, or the median formula M = l + ((N/2 − C)/f)·h
Compute M.D. = (1/N)Σf_i|x_i − (mean or median)|
misconceptions: Using a class limit instead of the class mid-point for x_i
Treating grouped data like a discrete list and skipping the median-class formula

nodes.title: Define variance as the mean of the squared deviations from the mean
nodes.brief: Define variance σ² = (1/n)Σ(x_i − x̄)², explaining why squaring deviations and then averaging gives a proper measure of dispersion.
nodes.quote: Another way to overcome this difficulty which arose due to the signs of deviations, is to take squares of all the deviations.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Squaring removes the sign problem.
Just as absolute values remove sign cancellation, squaring the deviations also makes them all non-negative: (x_i − x̄)² >= 0 for every i. Squaring has the additional algebraic benefit of being differentiable everywhere and easier to manipulate than absolute values in formal proofs.

PART 2 — Why we average the squared deviations.
The raw sum of squared deviations grows with the number of observations — a less scattered set of 100 values can yield a larger sum than a more scattered set of 5. To make the measure size-independent we divide by n, giving the variance: σ² = (1/n) times the sum of (x_i − x̄)².

PART 3 — Interpreting variance.
A variance of 0 means every observation equals the mean (no scatter). A larger σ² means more scatter. However, variance is in squared units of the original data (e.g. cm² if data are in cm), which is why we usually report the standard deviation instead.
keyMoves: Square each deviation (x_i − x̄)² to make it non-negative
Average the squared deviations rather than just summing them
Write σ² = (1/n)Σ(x_i − x̄)² as the variance
misconceptions: Using the raw sum Σ(x_i − x̄)² as the measure, ignoring the number of observations
Forgetting that squaring makes the cancellation of signs impossible (all terms non-negative)

nodes.title: Define standard deviation as the positive square root of the variance
nodes.brief: Define σ = √(variance) and explain why taking the square root restores the original units of the data.
nodes.quote: the proper measure of dispersion about the mean of a set of observations is expressed as positive square-root of the variance and is called *standard deviation*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The units problem with variance.
Variance σ² is in squared units of the original data. If the observations are measured in metres, the variance is in square metres — a unit that cannot be directly compared with the mean (in metres). This mismatch makes variance awkward to interpret on its own.

PART 2 — Standard deviation restores the original units.
We define the standard deviation σ as the positive square root of the variance: σ = sqrt(σ²) = sqrt((1/n) sum of (x_i − x̄)²). Since the square root undoes the squaring, σ is in the same units as the original data and can be compared directly with the mean.

PART 3 — Computing σ in practice.
Step 1: find x̄. Step 2: compute each squared deviation (x_i − x̄)². Step 3: average them to get σ². Step 4: take the positive square root. Example: data 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 have mean 6, squared deviations 16, 4, 0, 4, 16 summing to 40, so σ² = 40/5 = 8, and σ = sqrt(8) = 2sqrt(2).
keyMoves: Compute the variance σ² = (1/n)Σ(x_i − x̄)² first
Take the positive square root: σ = √(σ²)
Note σ carries the same units as the original data, unlike variance
misconceptions: Reporting the variance as if it were the standard deviation (forgetting the square root)
Taking the negative root, or treating σ as having squared units

nodes.title: Compute variance and standard deviation for frequency distributions
nodes.brief: Apply the frequency-weighted variance/SD formulas to discrete and continuous (mid-point) frequency distributions, including the computational formula via Σf_i x_i².
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Frequency-weighted variance formula.
For a frequency distribution with values (or mid-points) x_i and frequencies f_i, total N = sum of f_i, and mean x̄ = (1/N) sum of f_i x_i. The variance is σ² = (1/N) sum of f_i(x_i − x̄)². For continuous distributions substitute class mid-points for x_i first.

PART 2 — The computational (shortcut) formula.
Expanding (x_i − x̄)² and rearranging gives an equivalent formula that avoids computing individual deviations: σ² = (1/N) sum of f_i x_i² − x̄². Equivalently, σ = (1/N) sqrt( N times sum of f_i x_i² minus (sum of f_i x_i)² ). This is faster to evaluate from the tabulated f_i x_i and f_i x_i² columns.

PART 3 — Worked example.
For x_i = 3, 8, 13, 18, 23 with f_i = 7, 10, 15, 10, 6: N = 48, sum of f_i x_i = 614, sum of f_i x_i² = 9652. Then σ = (1/48) sqrt(48 times 9652 − 614²) = (1/48) sqrt(463296 − 376996) = (1/48) sqrt(86300) = (1/48) times 293.8 = 6.12.
keyMoves: Use mid-points for continuous classes, then x̄ = (1/N)Σf_i x_i
Compute σ² = (1/N)Σf_i(x_i − x̄)², σ = √(σ²)
Or use the computational form σ = (1/N)√[N·Σf_i x_i² − (Σf_i x_i)²]
misconceptions: Dropping the factor N inside the root in the computational formula
Forgetting to weight each squared deviation by its frequency f_i

nodes.title: Find variance and SD by the step-deviation (short-cut) method
nodes.brief: Use the change of origin and scale y_i = (x_i − A)/h to simplify large x_i, then recover σ via σ_x = h·σ_y.
nodes.quote: By using step-deviation method, it is possible to simplify the procedure.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The change of variable.
When the x_i (or class mid-points) are large, arithmetic is tedious. Choose an assumed mean A (usually the middle mid-point) and a scale h (usually the class width). Set y_i = (x_i − A)/h, so x_i = A + h y_i. The y_i are small integers such as −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3, which are far easier to square and sum.

PART 2 — How variance transforms.
Adding a constant A does not change any deviation x_i − x̄, so it does not change the variance. Multiplying by h scales every deviation by h and every squared deviation by h², so σ_x² = h² σ_y² and σ_x = h times σ_y. This is the key identity that lets us recover the original standard deviation from the simpler y-calculation.

PART 3 — The working formula.
Compute σ_y from the y_i values using the computational formula: σ_x = (h/N) sqrt(N times sum of f_i y_i² minus (sum of f_i y_i)²). Then σ_x is the answer for the original x data. The result is identical to the direct method but requires much less arithmetic.
keyMoves: Pick an assumed mean A and form y_i = (x_i − A)/h
Compute σ_y from the small y-values, then σ_x = h·σ_y
Equivalently σ_x = (h/N)√[N·Σf_i y_i² − (Σf_i y_i)²]
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply back by h (reporting σ_y instead of σ_x = h·σ_y)
Thinking the shift of origin by A changes the variance (it does not)

nodes.title: Predict how variance changes when observations are scaled or shifted
nodes.brief: Use the rules that adding a constant leaves the variance unchanged and multiplying by k multiplies the variance by k².
nodes.quote: the variance of the resulting observations becomes  $k^2$  times the original variance.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Shifting by a constant leaves variance unchanged.
If we add a constant a to every observation, forming y_i = x_i + a, then y̅ = x̄ + a and each deviation y_i − y̅ = (x_i + a) − (x̄ + a) = x_i − x̄. The deviations are identical, so the variance of the y_i equals the variance of the x_i. Adding or subtracting a constant does not change spread.

PART 2 — Scaling by k multiplies variance by k².
If we multiply every observation by k, forming y_i = k x_i, then y̅ = k x̄ and each deviation y_i − y̅ = k(x_i − x̄). Each squared deviation becomes k²(x_i − x̄)², so the variance of y is k² times the variance of x. The standard deviation of y is |k| times the standard deviation of x.

PART 3 — Combined linear change y = kx + a.
For a general linear transformation y_i = k x_i + a, both rules apply simultaneously: variance(y) = k² times variance(x). Only the scale factor k matters for variance; the shift a is irrelevant. This underpins the step-deviation method where y_i = (x_i − A)/h so k = 1/h, giving σ_x = h σ_y.
keyMoves: If each x_i is multiplied by k, multiply the variance by k² (SD by |k|)
If a constant a is added to each x_i, leave the variance unchanged
Combine the rules for a linear change y = kx + a (variance → k²·variance)
misconceptions: Multiplying the variance by k instead of k² when scaling the data
Thinking adding a constant changes the variance (it changes only the mean)

nodes.title: Choose and compute the right measure of dispersion to compare data sets
nodes.brief: Given any data set (ungrouped, discrete or continuous), select and compute range, mean deviation, variance or standard deviation to quantify and compare scatter.
nodes.quote: Range, Quartile deviation, mean deviation, variance, standard deviation are measures of dispersion.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Choosing a measure.
For a quick rough estimate use the range (max − min). When you need all observations to contribute and prefer to work in original units, use mean deviation M.D.(x̄) or M.D.(M). For the standard algebraic measure that is most used in further statistics, use variance σ² and standard deviation σ.

PART 2 — Adapting to data type.
For ungrouped data, apply formulas directly with n observations. For a discrete frequency distribution, weight every term by its frequency and divide by N = sum of f_i. For a continuous distribution, first replace each class by its mid-point, then proceed as for discrete. For large mid-points use the step-deviation shortcut y_i = (x_i − A)/h to reduce arithmetic.

PART 3 — Comparing two data sets.
Two sets with the same mean can be compared by their standard deviation (or variance): the set with the smaller σ is more consistent (less spread). The scale/shift rules — variance unchanged by adding a constant, multiplied by k² when data are scaled by k — let you correct or transform data efficiently. These tools together allow a complete comparative analysis of any two data sets.
keyMoves: Identify the data type (ungrouped, discrete, continuous) and pick a suitable measure
Compute range, mean deviation, variance or standard deviation as appropriate
Compare two data sets by their dispersion, and use scale/shift or step-deviation rules to simplify
misconceptions: Comparing only means/medians and ignoring dispersion when judging consistency
Mixing up the per-data-type formulas (weighting by f_i, using mid-points, N vs n)
